meta.title: Protector AI Stream — Web Camera Link
meta.description: Connect your RTSP camera and get a public link for the inspector. Cloud video surveillance for industrial facilities.

hero.eyebrow: Cloud video surveillance for industrial facilities
hero.lede: We connect your camera or DVR and provide a public link that the inspector opens in a browser without installing any software.
hero.trustline: Suitable for online video surveillance of production facilities and sharing links with inspection authorities.
hero.cardCaption: ↑ This is what the inspector sees after opening the link

demo.sub: Press play and see how the link looks to the inspector.
demo.sideTitle: The same player the inspector will see
demo.sideBody: The demo uses the same Low-Latency HLS core as the public live page. Here we test playback, fallback, and error states in your browser.
demo.stateStartingSub: Receiving first segments from the camera.
demo.statePlayTitle: Click Play to start the demo
demo.statePlaySub: Browser requires confirmation before playing video.
demo.stateOfflineSub: The demo camera is temporarily offline. Try refreshing in a minute.
demo.stateErrorSub: Browser couldn't open the stream. This can happen on older Safari versions or when the network is blocked.
demo.stateDescLive: Stream is playing in real time
demo.stateDescError: Browser failed to play the stream

problemSolution.sub: Cameras speak one protocol. Browsers and government portals speak another. We bridge the gap.
problemSolution.problemH3: RTSP doesn't open in a browser
problemSolution.problemP: Most IP cameras and DVRs stream over RTSP. Such a link works in VLC or special software, but doesn't open directly in a browser and is inconvenient for the inspector.
problemSolution.solutionH3: We turn the camera stream into a web link
problemSolution.solutionP: The service takes the RTSP stream, prepares it for the browser, and provides a public page with the facility description and a video player.

onboarding.sub: We handle all setup and connection. All you need is access to the facility.
onboarding.step1Body: First, we find out how the network is set up at the facility: where the cameras or DVR are, whether there is a public IP address, who manages the router, and whether video streams can be safely exposed.
onboarding.step2Title: We connect cameras the right way
onboarding.step2Body: If you have a public IP address, we configure access and publish cameras as a secure web link. If there is no public IP, we install our device and set up a private VPN tunnel.
onboarding.step3Title: You get links in your dashboard
onboarding.step3Body: After setup, links to connected cameras appear in your dashboard. You can send the link to the inspector — they will open a page with the facility description and a video player.
onboarding.mode1Body: We configure access to cameras or NVR, check RTSP streams, and publish them as web links.
onboarding.mode2Body: We install our device at the facility and connect it to the platform via a private VPN tunnel.
onboarding.mode3Body: After setup, the user sees a list of cameras, statuses, limits, and public links.

features.sub: A simple set of tools for one task — showing the camera to the inspector.
features.feature1Body: The link opens without login and shows only safe information.
features.feature2Body: Live and offline status display in your dashboard.
features.feature3Body: We help with camera setup and connection.

pricing.sub: One package for MVP. No complex billing — we will add volume plans later.
pricing.item2: Up to 5 simultaneous viewers per camera
pricing.item4: Dashboard with camera list and statuses
pricing.item5: Connection via public IP or WireGuard

faq.sub: In short — yes, we do it. Details below.
faq.q1: Do I need a public IP?
faq.a1: Not always. If you have a public IP, you can connect the camera or DVR directly via RTSP. If not, a WireGuard tunnel is used — our device is installed at the facility and sets up a private channel to the platform.
faq.q2: Where do I get the camera's RTSP link?
faq.a2: The RTSP link can be found in the camera or DVR settings. It usually contains login, password, IP address, and stream path. The format depends on the manufacturer — we help find the link for popular brands.
faq.a3: The inspector will open a public camera page with the company description, camera details, and a video player. No login required, no special software — just a regular web link in the browser.
faq.a4: On a normal network, the target latency is about 1–3 seconds. On a poor network, latency may be higher. We use Low-Latency HLS — a standard supported by modern browsers.
faq.q5: Can I limit the number of viewers?
faq.a5: Yes. By default, one camera allows up to 5 simultaneous viewers. The limit can be changed on request — contact support.
faq.q6: Is there a viewing time limit?
faq.a6: Yes. By default, each camera has a monthly limit of 100 stream-hours. The limit is counted by stream runtime, not by the number of viewers.
faq.q7: Is it safe to share camera credentials?
faq.a7: RTSP logins and passwords are stored encrypted. We do not expose credentials or share them with third parties. The public link contains only the video stream — with no access to camera settings.

contact.h3: Leave a request — we will help connect your camera.
contact.lede: Tell us about your facility — we will advise on the connection method, check your camera, and help set up the inspector link.
contact.successMsg: Request submitted. We will contact you and advise on how to connect your camera.
contact.errorMsg: Please enter a phone number — we will call you back.
contact.errorMsgEmail: Please enter a valid email address.
contact.errorMsgApi: Failed to send. Please try again or contact us directly.

footer.note: Web link to camera for online video surveillance of production facilities.
